title: Lila Games APM Case Study
problem: The mobile gaming industry in 2025 is converging across PC, console, and mobile experiences, with AI and shifting player behavior reshaping how games are built, marketed, and monetized. Lila Games needed a scalable art production model for a new COD Zombies-style mobile title, alongside a cross-genre creative research framework to inform user acquisition strategy for two upcoming titles LILA Black (Casual Extraction Shooter) and Heart's Desire (Dating Sim). The challenge required translating fast-moving market signals into structured, data-backed production and marketing decisions rather than relying on assumptions.
approach:
A three-part strategic framework was built to address production planning, creative research, and applied UA strategy in parallel.

Art Pipeline: A fully formula-driven Google Sheets model was built to estimate art asset requirements for a 9-month development cycle plus 6-month soft launch, grounded in genre benchmarking (8-12 zombie types, 12-16 weapon variants, modular environment design) rather than arbitrary estimates

Staffing Model: Total production hours were calculated bottom-up from asset volume and complexity, then converted into role-based headcount requirements (Concept, 3D Character, 3D Environment, VFX, UI, Animation) and validated against a 9-month capacity constraint

Creative Research Framework: A repeatable analysis structure (hook, story/emotion, visual style, format, CTA, localization) was applied consistently across three distinct genres shooter (Free Fire), dating sim (Episode), and puzzle (Merge Mansion) using verified market data, agency case studies, and industry benchmarks

Applied UA Strategy: Cross-genre findings were translated into genre-specific creative concept directions for LILA Black, each tied directly to a research-backed performance pattern rather than a subjective creative pitch

Competitive Art Benchmarking: Battle pass art direction was analyzed across five live games (Fortnite Mobile, Brawl Stars, COD Mobile, Arena Breakout, BGMI) using a consistent lens: art strategy, purchase psychology, and competitive differentiation
result: A scalable, formula-based art production model was delivered that automatically adjusts staffing and timeline feasibility as asset scope changes, alongside a cross-genre creative insights framework directly applied to Lila's own upcoming titles. The output translated market research into a concrete production plan and genre-specific UA creative direction, aligning Lila's roadmap decisions with current 2025 gaming industry trends in art strategy, monetization, and player acquisition.
metrics: 7,440 total art-production hours modeled and validated against team capacity across a 9-month timeline
5 live games benchmarked in the battle pass art strategy analysis
3 gaming genres analyzed using verified campaign performance data
20+ primary sources (market intelligence tools, agency case studies, industry reports) used to validate every recommendation

title: Accredian Student Communication Dashboard Case Study
problem: Accredian students rely on external apps like WhatsApp, Telegram, and LinkedIn to stay connected with their cohort, coordinate group work, and share resources. Because communication is scattered across disconnected platforms, students miss important updates, struggle to separate academic messages from personal chats, and find it difficult to build genuine peer connections within the learning environment. This creates a disjointed learning experience where the platform students are enrolled on isn't where their actual collaboration happens.
approach:
A single, centralized communication hub was designed to bring cohort collaboration, peer discovery, and community engagement back inside Accredian, replacing the need for external apps entirely.

Research and Competitive Analysis: WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and Slack were benchmarked against student communication needs, identifying that no existing tool combines structured course linked chat, peer discovery, lightweight productivity, and community engagement in one product

Behavioral Research Grounding: Design decisions were backed by academic and UX research on threaded discussion clarity, AI summarization benefits, learning analytics dashboard engagement, and community driven retention in online learning

Feature Architecture: Eight core screens were designed, including Home Dashboard, Cohort Chat, Study Group, Communities, Fun Zone, Voice Rooms, Peer Discovery, and Personal Notes, each mapped to a specific student pain point

Trade off Decisions: Pastel UI was chosen over traditional LMS design to reduce cognitive load; threaded chat was chosen over free flow chat to reduce noise in large cohorts; a non academic Fun Zone was deliberately included to drive engagement and community culture

Risk Planning: Key adoption risks such as habit stickiness to WhatsApp, community spam, feature overload, and Fun Zone overuse were identified with corresponding mitigation strategies built into the design
result: A complete, research backed product concept was delivered showing how Accredian could consolidate five plus fragmented communication channels into a single, student centric hub. The prototype demonstrates a scalable interaction model, from structured chat to peer discovery to lightweight productivity, designed to shift student behavior away from external apps toward a centralized, learning integrated communication experience.
metrics: 8 fully designed product screens covering communication, collaboration, and community use cases
4 competitor platforms benchmarked, including WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and Slack
10+ academic and industry research sources cited to ground UX and engagement decisions in evidence
4 identified adoption risks paired with specific mitigation strategies
Fully modular design system, allowing sections to be expanded or replaced independently as the platform scales

title: VibeCircle Product Strategy Case Study
problem: Young creators and users in Tier-2 and Tier-3 Indian cities are underserved by mainstream social platforms, which are built for metro, English-first, high-follower-count creator economies. Local creators struggle to earn even small amounts because platforms require large follower thresholds, local content gets buried under national viral trends, and cultural or language barriers make users feel like outsiders on their own apps. On top of this, technical friction such as failed OTP sign-ups on slow networks, lack of low-data modes, and unresolved harassment reports pushes users toward informal channels like WhatsApp instead of staying inside a dedicated platform.
approach:
A full product strategy was developed to address earning, discovery, safety, and technical accessibility gaps for Tier-2/3 users through a single mobile-first platform.

Problem Mapping: User problems were segmented into two categories, monetization and cultural exclusion versus app experience and safety, to separate economic pain points from platform usability issues

Use Case Analysis: Four recurring user scenarios were defined, including the Weekend Planner, the Hobbyist Hunt, the Creator's Crisis, and the Onboarding Trap, to ground feature decisions in real behavioral patterns rather than assumptions

Market Sizing: TAM was estimated at 120 million users aged 16 to 25 in Tier-2/3 cities, with a focused segment of 50 million users already sharing content externally due to lack of in-app community

Solution Architecture: Seven core solution pillars were designed, including a local city algorithm, interest groups, a micro-earning marketplace, regional language safety filters, offline/low-data mode, and WhatsApp-based OTP sign-up

Growth Strategy: A three-phase adoption plan was structured across seed, viral scale, and national presence stages, targeting 10 million users in year one through college seeding, WhatsApp referral loops, and telecom pre-installation partnerships

Risk Planning: Six risk categories were identified, spanning content trust, safety, competitive, technical, scalability, and adoption risks, each paired with a specific mitigation strategy
result: A complete go-to-market product strategy was delivered for a hyperlocal, creator-first social platform, translating underserved user pain points into a structured feature set, phased growth plan, and risk-mitigated execution roadmap. The prototype demonstrates how micro-earning, local discovery, and safety-first design can be combined to shift Tier-2/3 users from fragmented, externally-hosted communities into a single dedicated platform built around their specific economic and cultural needs.
metrics: 120 million user TAM identified across Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities
50 million user segment identified as high-intent early adopters already sharing content externally
10 million user adoption target set for year one across a three-phase growth plan
7 core product features designed to directly address mapped user problems
6 risk categories addressed with corresponding mitigation plans
14-screen wireframe prototype built covering onboarding, home feed, groups, marketplace, safety, offline mode, and growth features

title: Transaction Reconciliation - Fintech Product Case Study
problem: Finance teams at investment firms rely on a daily reconciliation tool to match internal ledger data against external bank and broker feeds, but the tool's exact-match logic flags harmless formatting differences, like "Acme Corp" versus "Acme Corporation," as full mismatches. Analysts spend most of their day manually sorting through hundreds of exceptions with no priority order, no way to assign work across a team, no audit trail explaining why a match was approved, and no logic to distinguish real errors from routine settlement timing lags. This creates daily operational drag, increases audit and compliance risk, and pushes analysts to check everything by hand, defeating the purpose of having the tool at all.
approach:
A three-phase improvement plan was proposed to fix matching accuracy, prioritization, and auditability, with each phase building on the one before it.

User Workflow Analysis: A day-in-the-life walkthrough of a reconciliation analyst was mapped to identify exactly where time was lost, including manual file uploads, exact-match false breaks, unranked exception lists, and undocumented approval decisions

Smart Matching Engine: A rules-based scoring model was designed to evaluate amount, date within a one-day window, reference number, counterparty name using flexible text matching, and currency, generating a 0 to 100 confidence score with plain-English reasoning for each suggested match

Exception Triage Queue: A structured panel was designed to auto-categorize exceptions by type such as amount mismatch, missing record, timing difference, or duplicate, and auto-rank them by financial risk and age, with automatic closure logic for routine settlement lags

Audit Trail and Explainability: A field-by-field comparison log was designed to record who approved each match, when, under which rule, and with what score breakdown, exportable directly for compliance and audit review

Engineering Collaboration Plan: A working-session approach was defined instead of a document handoff, including specific technical questions on data normalization, transaction volume limits, one-to-many batch matching, and decision-logging for future model tuning

Edge Case Planning: Six data risk scenarios were identified and addressed, including duplicate transactions, time zone mismatches, one-to-many batch settlements, decimal precision mismatches, and blank reference fields

Rollout and Validation Plan: A parallel-run testing approach was proposed across two full reconciliation cycles, tracking auto-match rate, false positive rate, and false negative rate before full production rollout, with a feature flag for instant rollback
result: A complete product improvement proposal was delivered for a fintech reconciliation platform, translating a high-friction manual workflow into a phased plan covering smart matching, risk-based triage, and compliance-ready audit trails. The proposal includes concrete wireframe direction, user stories, engineering collaboration steps, and a measurable rollout validation plan, demonstrating how confidence-scored automation can reduce analyst workload while keeping every decision explainable for regulatory audit.
metrics: 3 phased improvements proposed, each targeting a distinct part of the reconciliation workflow
90%+ target auto-match rate benchmarked against industry-leading reconciliation platforms
Below 0.1% target false positive rate defined as the primary early-rollout success threshold
6 edge case scenarios identified and addressed before engineering handoff
5 technical questions defined for engineering feasibility discussion
2 full reconciliation cycles proposed for parallel-run validation before production cutover
Up to 50% potential operations time savings referenced from industry research on ML-assisted reconciliation

title: Cloud Posture Overview Dashboard Case Study
problem: Security teams managing cloud environments across AWS, Azure, and GCP lack a unified view to see and act on misconfigurations such as open storage buckets, weak access rules, or missing encryption. Findings are scattered across accounts, ownership is unclear, and inconsistent tagging creates noise that delays remediation. This fragmented visibility increases the risk of data breaches and compliance failures, and makes it difficult for teams to measure progress or prioritize the issues that matter most.
approach:
A three-layer dashboard workflow was designed to move users from high-level posture trends down to specific, actionable findings.

Persona Definition: Two primary users were identified, a time-constrained Cloud Security Analyst who needs fast filters and clear evidence, and a Security Engineering Lead who needs trend visibility, SLAs, and hot-spot account tracking

Information Architecture: A three-step workflow was structured as Overview for posture trends, List for specific misconfigurations, and Details for resolution and compliance mapping, so users move from summary to action without losing context

Feature Design: Core features were defined including a unified multi-account KPI view, a filterable findings list by severity, provider, account, service, and tags, detailed finding views with evidence and remediation steps, and an assignment and suppression system with full audit trail

Prioritization Framework: Features were split into MVP, next iteration, and stretch goals, with MVP covering overview KPIs, filterable lists, finding details, and assign or suppress actions, while stretch goals included one-click remediation and automated risk scoring

Engineering Action Planning: Specific backend, frontend, governance, and observability requirements were defined, including finding normalization across cloud providers, RBAC, suppression policy with expiry rules, and usage telemetry across the overview to detail funnel
result: A complete cloud security posture dashboard concept was delivered, translating scattered, multi-account misconfiguration data into a single prioritized workflow for identification, assignment, and remediation tracking. The design demonstrates measurable posture improvement through severity-based prioritization and audit-ready remediation tracking, giving both analysts and engineering leads a shared, actionable view of organizational cloud risk.
metrics: 275 total findings tracked, reflecting a 12% increase period over period
29 critical and high severity findings, reflecting an 8% decrease period over period
47 accounts affected across monitored cloud environments
4.2 days mean time to remediate, reflecting an 18% improvement in remediation speed
3 cloud providers supported in a single unified view, including AWS, Azure, and GCP
3-tier feature prioritization defined across MVP, next iteration, and stretch scope

title: AI Mentor for UPSC Aspirants Case Study
problem: India's government exam prep market serves 30 plus million aspirants annually, yet selection rates remain below 1 to 3 percent despite an explosion of free digital content. Research shows dropouts are driven less by lack of intelligence and more by behavioral fatigue, poor feedback loops, and declining motivation over the 2 to 4 year prep cycle. Aspirants suffer from content abundance but direction scarcity, spending more time collecting PDFs and notes than actually studying, waiting months for feedback on their readiness, and losing motivation in isolation without the accountability of a physical coaching environment.
approach:
A syllabus-aware AI mentor was designed as a Success Operating System rather than another content platform, focused on solving behavior and direction rather than adding more material.

Persona Focus: The MVP was scoped around Tier 2/3 city aspirants, first and second attempt self-study candidates, and busy working professionals preparing part-time, since these segments are most underserved by existing content-heavy, urban-focused apps

Core Product Decision: An AI-driven mentor and feedback engine was chosen over a pure planning tool or community platform, directly targeting the feedback gap and collection trap identified as the two biggest drivers of aspirant dropout

MVP Scope: Features were defined around eliminating PDF hunting through auto-generated syllabus notes, closing the feedback gap through a real-time progress dashboard, and reducing motivation decay through daily gamified questions and peer-based accountability

Differentiation Framing: The product was positioned against three categories of existing solutions, replacing static notes and videos with AI-driven syllabus content, replacing generic mock tests with dynamically generated exam-trend-aligned questions, and replacing scattered news with exam-relevance-mapped current affairs

Monetization and GTM Strategy: A freemium model was proposed with coin-based gamification driving premium conversion, supported by institution partnerships for bulk licensing and an ad-lite option for free users, with go-to-market built through student communities, UPSC topper collaborations, and a gamified referral loop rather than paid brand marketing

Risk and Trade-off Planning: Five key risks were identified, including AI accuracy on syllabus content, gamification balance against serious prep intent, peer misinformation risk, low-bandwidth feature trade-offs, and adoption resistance from aspirants loyal to established coaching brands
result: A complete founding PM pitch was delivered for an AI-driven exam prep mentor, translating a large but underserved aspirant market into a focused MVP that addresses behavioral dropout drivers rather than adding more content. The proposal defines a clear path from problem identification to monetization and GTM execution, positioning the product as a feedback-first, low-bandwidth alternative to existing content-heavy, urban-centric prep platforms.
metrics: 30+ million addressable aspirants across UPSC, SSC, Banking, Railways, and State PSC exams
1 to 3% current selection rate identified as the core outcome gap the product aims to close
3 core user problems mapped directly to MVP feature decisions, including the collection trap, feedback gap, and motivation decay
9 MVP features scoped across AI mentoring, content generation, progress tracking, and peer engagement
5 monetization levers defined across freemium, premium, gamified coins, institutional licensing, and ad-lite models
5 risk categories identified with specific trade-off reasoning for a seed-stage resource-constrained build
